title: Security findings should identify the trust boundary and the safer construction pattern together
text: Keep this as reference-only policy so cross-language security comments stay actionable without forcing a defect claim when system context is incomplete.
summary: Shared security guidance is more useful when it teaches reconstruction, not just detection.

title: Prefer findings with direct evidence from the changed code
text: Review findings should stay tightly grounded in the changed code. When a concern depends on missing repository context or multiple speculative assumptions, keep it as reference-only guidance instead of an inline defect claim.
summary: Publish only when the patch itself provides concrete evidence of the issue.

title: Keep fix guidance actionable and bounded
text: A good review comment explains the failure mode and points to a safe repair direction. Avoid vague suggestions that force the author to infer the missing design.
summary: Suggested fixes should be concrete enough for the author to act on without guessing the intended direction.

title: Findings should name the boundary that was crossed, not just the smell that was noticed
text: Prefer review language that ties the concern to ownership, resource lifetime, input trust, concurrency, or another explicit boundary instead of only naming a generic anti-pattern.
summary: Review comments are more actionable when they describe the concrete contract or trust boundary at risk.

title: Security comments should identify the untrusted input and safer construction pattern
text: When raising a security issue, point to the specific untrusted input, privilege boundary, or executable surface involved and suggest the construction pattern that would narrow it.
summary: A useful security finding explains both the boundary and the safer way to build the code.

title: Escalate uncertainty as guidance when repository-wide proof is missing
text: Keep defect findings grounded in changed-code evidence. When a concern depends on unshown configuration, runtime state, or external call patterns, phrase it as bounded guidance instead of a hard defect assertion.
summary: Reference guidance is better than a false inline defect claim when the patch alone cannot prove the bug.

title: Findings should explain the safer reconstruction pattern, not only name the smell
text: This guidance is shared across languages and is best kept as reference-only process policy rather than an inline defect rule.
summary: Authors move faster when the comment points to the safer boundary shape instead of only criticizing the current line.

title: When context is partial, prefer bounded guidance over speculative system-wide claims
text: Use this process rule to keep large multi-language reviews useful and trustworthy even when only one changed file of a broader system is visible.
summary: High-confidence review depends on staying close to what the patch itself proves.

title: API boundaries should choose one absence contract and keep it consistent
text: This is often better treated as reference guidance unless the patch shows an immediate caller hazard.
summary: Null, Optional, empty collection, and exception-based absence models should not drift casually across similar APIs.

title: Resource, concurrency, and null handling are part of the public API contract, not just internal implementation detail
text: Use reference guidance when the changed code suggests unclear boundary behavior but the correctness of the full contract depends on surrounding code.
summary: Java review often benefits from a contract-level note even when the patch alone cannot prove a direct defect.

title: Shared mutable collection contracts should name the synchronization owner and iteration boundary
text: Use this as reference guidance when the code suggests a mutable shared collection contract but the full synchronization story lives across multiple callers, threads, or lifecycle owners.
summary: Concurrent collection guidance often needs wider design context than the local patch exposes.
